title: The One With All The Poker

expressions.dialogue: Uh, Rach, we're **running low on** resumes over here.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 공급이 부족해지다, 거의 다 떨어져 가다
expressions.nuance: 'We're out of ~'는 이미 다 떨어진 것이고, 'running low on ~'은 아직 조금 남아 있지만 빠르게 줄고 있다는 뉘앙스. 음식, 물품, 시간, 에너지 등 다양하게 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: We're running low on coffee — can you grab another bag? → 커피가 거의 다 떨어져가고 있어. 한 봉지 더 사올 수 있어?

expressions.dialogue: Hey, look, you guys, I'm **going for** anything here, OK?
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 무엇이든 가리지 않고 시도하다, 닥치는 대로 도전하다
expressions.nuance: 'go for'는 무언가를 적극적으로 추구하는 것. 'I'm going for anything'은 선택지를 가리지 않겠다는 절박함이 담긴 표현으로, 취업 준비나 절박한 상황에서 자주 나온다.
expressions.example: I've been job hunting for three months — I'm going for anything at this point. → 세 달째 구직 중이야. 이젠 뭐든 닥치는 대로 지원하고 있어.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Please, I'm not going for anything.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: No, God no! That is no place for a woman. Those guys will grab anything.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: I got this pair marked excess, I gotta tell ya, there was no room for excess anything in there.

expressions.dialogue: I'm **sick of** the lousy tips, I'm **sick of** being called 'Excuse me...'
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.nuance: 'tired of'보다 감정이 훨씬 강하다. 반복되는 상황에 더 이상 참기 힘들 때 쓰는 표현으로, 말할 때 짜증 섞인 어조가 자연스럽다.
expressions.example: I'm sick of this commute. I'm moving closer to work. → 이 출퇴근에 완전 질렸어. 직장 근처로 이사할 거야.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: It's just that I'm sick of being a victim of this Dick Clark holiday. I say this year, no dates, we make a pact. Just the six of us- dinner.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: I am so sick of guys. I don't want to look at another guy, I don't want to think about another guy, I don't even want to be near another guy. (Ross crosses arms)

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Ooh, I, I'm so sick of missing stuff. Ya know, I want him for more than, than a day, I want him for a whole weekend. No listen , I mean, I feel like-

expressions.dialogue: Linda is so great! Why won't you **go out with** her again?
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.nuance: 'date someone'과 같은 의미로 자연스럽게 쓰인다. 1회성 데이트와 연인 관계 모두를 가리킬 수 있어 문맥으로 구분해야 한다.
expressions.example: She finally said yes when I asked her to go out with me. → 같이 나가자고 했더니 드디어 OK 했어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: C'mon, you're going out with the guy! There's gotta be something wrong with him!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Wait a minute! We're talking about someone that I'm going out with?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What's going on here? You go out with tons of girls.

expressions.dialogue: I want someone who... who **gets my heart pounding**, who... who makes me...
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.nuance: 로맨틱한 설렘을 묘사할 때 특히 자주 쓰인다. 흥분이나 긴장에도 쓸 수 있지만, 이 맥락에서는 사랑의 두근거림이다.
expressions.example: That movie scene still gets my heart pounding every time I watch it. → 그 영화 장면은 볼 때마다 심장이 두근거려.

expressions.dialogue: Look, I am **totally, totally over** her, OK, I just...
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 누군가에 대한 감정이 완전히 식었다, 완전히 잊었다
expressions.nuance: 'over someone'은 전 연인이나 짝사랑 대상에 대한 미련이 없다는 것. 'totally'를 붙이면 강조인데, 의심받을 때 과도하게 부정하는 뉘앙스로도 쓰인다 — Ross처럼.
expressions.example: Don't worry, I'm totally over him. It's been two years. → 걱정 마, 그 사람 완전히 잊었어. 벌써 2년이 됐는걸.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I'll bet he's totally over me, I'll bet he's fine.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: You totally screwed him over.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: You're right, I have no excuses! I was totally over the line.

expressions.dialogue: Is that like, **some kind of guy thing**? Like, some kind of sexist guy thing?
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: ~만의 특성, ~에 관한 어떤 것 (정확히 뭔지 모르겠지만)
expressions.nuance: 정확히 뭔지 모르지만 그 집단 특유의 무언가를 가리킬 때 쓴다. 약간 비판적이거나 의아한 톤으로 쓰일 때가 많다.
expressions.example: Is this some kind of hazing thing, or do you treat all new employees this way? → 이게 신고식 같은 건가요, 아니면 신입 사원을 다 이렇게 대하는 건가요?

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Listen. Um, listen. Something you should... know... um, Rachel and I... we're kind of a thing.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: I think when someone steals your credit card, they've kind of already thrown caution to the wind.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Well, maybe he had some kind of uh, new, cool style, that you're not familiar with. And uh maybe you have to get used to it.

expressions.dialogue: Oh, please, **that is such a lame excuse**!
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.nuance: 'lame'은 구어에서 '형편없는, 설득력 없는'이라는 뜻으로 쓰인다. 상대방의 핑계를 단호히 일축할 때 쓰는 표현.
expressions.example: You didn't come because you were 'tired'? That's a lame excuse. → '피곤해서' 못 왔다고? 그건 너무 핑계 같은데.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (disgusted) Limited seating! Oh, that is such a lame excuse! That's not the reason she's not inviting me!

expressions.dialogue: We **just don't happen to** know any women that know how to play poker.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.nuance: 'happen to'는 의도하지 않았지만 우연히 그렇게 됐다는 뉘앙스. 여기서는 변명처럼 쓰이고 있어 살짝 아이러니컬하다.
expressions.example: I just happened to run into my ex at the grocery store. → 마트에서 전 남자친구를 우연히 마주쳤어.

expressions.dialogue: Rach, Rach, we **gotta settle**.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 게임이나 활동 후 돈 계산을 마무리하다, 정산하다
expressions.nuance: 'settle' 또는 'settle up'은 빚이나 계산을 마무리한다는 의미. 게임, 식사, 공동 구매 후 각자 몫을 정산할 때 쓴다.
expressions.example: Before you leave, we need to settle up for last night's dinner. → 가기 전에 어젯밤 저녁 값 정산해야 해.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Y'know, I don't think we're going to settle this.

expressions.dialogue: **Hell no**, we'll pay!
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.nuance: 'No'를 강하게 표현하는 비속어적 표현. 여기서는 '돈 내지 않아도 된다'는 제안에 강하게 반박하는 것으로, 친한 사이에서는 자주 쓰인다.
expressions.example: Are you gonna let him talk to you like that? Hell no. → '그가 그런 식으로 말하게 둘 거야?' '절대 안 되지.'

occ.speaker: Carol
occ.text: Hello? It's not gonna be Helen Geller.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Nooo! (She grabs the phone and Chandler takes her place on the mat.) (On phone) Hello? (Listens) Oh, yeah, no, I know, I-I haven't been using it much. (Listens) Oh, well, thanks, but, I'm okay, really.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh, then no thanks. What the hell was that? Mental note: If Jill Goodacre offers you gum, you take it. If she offers you mangled animal carcass, you take it.

expressions.dialogue: That's fine with me. **Could use** the money.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.nuance: 'I need ~'보다 훨씬 캐주얼하고 부드러운 표현. 직접적인 요청이 아니라 '있으면 좋지'라는 뉘앙스로, 더 자연스러운 회화체.
expressions.example: I could use a vacation right now. → 지금 당장 휴가가 좀 필요한 것 같아.

occ.speaker: Ronni
occ.text: Yeah, uh, Joey said I could use your shower, since, uh, Chandler's in ours?

occ.speaker: Janice
occ.text: Oh, are you sure? Really? Because you know, you could make little puppets out of them, and you could use them in your theater of cruelty.

occ.speaker: Jamie
occ.text: This could be God's way of telling us to eat at home.

expressions.dialogue: So basically, you **get your ya-yas** by taking money from all of your friends.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.nuance: 1990년대 미국 구어 표현으로, 'get one's kicks'나 'get one's jollies'와 비슷한 뜻. 어딘가에서 특이한 방식으로 만족을 취하는 것을 약간 비꼬는 뉘앙스로 쓸 때가 많다.
expressions.example: He gets his ya-yas from arguing with strangers online. → 그 사람은 온라인에서 모르는 사람들이랑 싸우는 걸 즐기더라.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: (Getting up and raising his fist in victory) Y-Y-YEEESSS! Make "groom" for Chandler.

expressions.dialogue: Oh, **I beg to differ**. The Pictionary incident?
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.nuance: 격식체에서 온 표현이지만 일상 대화에서도 쓰인다. 'I disagree'보다 점잖지만 실제로는 꽤 강하게 반박할 때 쓴다.
expressions.example: You said the meeting went well, but I beg to differ — half the team left unhappy. → 회의가 잘 됐다고 하셨는데, 저는 동의하기 어렵습니다. 팀원 절반이 불만을 갖고 나갔거든요.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: I beg to differ (shows him her cup of coffee and her plate of cookies).

expressions.dialogue: Hmm. **Kinda stepped on my point** there, Mon.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 상대방의 말이나 포인트를 덮어버리다, 효과를 망쳐버리다
expressions.nuance: 인상적인 말을 하려는데 다른 사람이 더 과격하거나 강한 말을 해버려 효과가 사라질 때 쓴다. 'steal someone's thunder'와 비슷한 느낌.
expressions.example: I was about to make that same point, but you totally stepped on it. → 나도 그 말 하려던 참이었는데 네가 덮어버렸잖아.

expressions.dialogue: So, you gals wanna **hand over** your money now?
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.nuance: 'give'보다 약간 더 강제적이거나 공식적인 뉘앙스. 항복하거나 양도하는 상황에서 자주 쓰인다.
expressions.example: The robber told him to hand over his wallet. → 강도가 그에게 지갑을 내놓으라고 했다.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Okay, Pheebs: Right hand blue. (Phoebe has to bend over.)Good. (Joey stares at her butt appreciatively)

occ.speaker: Nurse
occ.text: You are an idiot. (She hands over a blank form).

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: (talking in her sleep) Oooooooooh. (Rachel strokes her hand over the pillow. Ross mimicks her silently) Oh, that's nice. Oh, oh. Huh, Ross!

expressions.dialogue: That way, we don't have to **go through the formality of** actually playing.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: ~의 형식적 절차를 밟다 (결과가 뻔할 때 굳이 과정을 밟는다는 뉘앙스)
expressions.nuance: 결과가 이미 정해진 것처럼 생각될 때 '굳이 그 과정을 밟을 필요가 있냐'는 뉘앙스로 쓴다. 살짝 건방지거나 자신감 넘치는 표현.
expressions.example: We all know you're getting the promotion — do we really need to go through the formality of an interview? → 네가 승진할 거라는 건 다 알잖아. 굳이 면접이라는 형식을 밟아야 할까?

expressions.dialogue: Boy, you really **can't stand to lose**, can you?
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 지는 걸 참지 못하다, 패배를 못 견디다
expressions.nuance: 'can't stand to ~'는 '~을 견딜 수 없다'는 표현. 지는 상황 자체를 못 받아들이는 성격을 묘사할 때 쓴다.
expressions.example: He can't stand to lose at anything — even board games make him upset. → 그 사람은 뭐든 지는 걸 못 참아. 보드게임에서 져도 화를 내.

expressions.dialogue: I mean, what, **were you just full of it**?
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 허풍을 떨다, 헛소리를 하다, 뻥을 치다
expressions.nuance: 'full of sh*t'의 완화된 표현. 누군가의 말이 믿기 어렵거나 허세로 가득 차 있을 때 쓴다. 직설적이지만 비속어보다는 덜 공격적이다.
expressions.example: He said he could bench 300 pounds, but he's totally full of it. → 그가 벤치프레스 300파운드 든다고 했는데, 완전 뻥이야.

occ.speaker: Mr. Kaplan
occ.text: (opening the closet door revealing that it's full of tangled up hangers.) I need these hangers separated ASAP. (she is stunned) You're welcome.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Fungus! Yeah. Place is full of it.

expressions.dialogue: Oh, guys, you should've seen him. '**Read 'em and weep.**'
expressions.speaker: Ross (quoting Joey)
expressions.meaning: 패배를 인정하고 울어라 (이길 것이 확실한 패를 공개할 때 하는 말)
expressions.nuance: 포커에서 상대방에게 자신의 이기는 패를 보여주며 하는 말. 일상에서도 확실한 승리나 압도적인 결과를 자랑할 때 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: Read 'em and weep — I got a perfect score on the exam. → 봐라, 부럽지? 나 이번 시험 만점 받았어.

expressions.dialogue: **You're kidding!** Where? Where?
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.nuance: 'Are you serious?', 'No way!', 'Really?'와 비슷한 표현. 긍정적인 놀라움과 부정적인 놀라움 모두에 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: They're giving out free concert tickets at the plaza! — You're kidding! Let's go! → '광장에서 콘서트 티켓 무료로 나눠준대!' '설마! 빨리 가자!'

occ.speaker: Frannie
occ.text: Are you kidding? I take credit for Paul. Y'know before me, there was no snap in his turtle for two years.

occ.speaker: All
occ.text: You're kidding. Oh my God.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: No, you kidding? The guy's a freak.. (Ross enters off camera)

expressions.dialogue: Ha, **I made you look**...
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.nuance: 상대방을 거짓 정보로 속여 고개를 돌리게 했을 때 하는 말. 주로 장난기 있는 상황에서 쓰며, Rachel이 Ross에게 '지퍼 열렸다'고 속인 뒤에 쓴 표현이다.
expressions.example: Look, your shoelace is untied! — Ha, I made you look. → '야, 신발끈 풀렸어!' '하, 속았지!'

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: It made you feel good, so that makes it selfish. Look, there's no unselfish good deeds, sorry.

expressions.dialogue: **Couldn't be more** out.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.nuance: 어떤 상태의 극단을 표현할 때 쓴다. 'I'm completely out'이나 'I'm totally out'보다 훨씬 생동감 있고 자연스러운 표현.
expressions.example: I couldn't be more excited about this trip. → 이번 여행이 너무너무 기대돼.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Uh, well yeah-yeah, I've got all of that going on. Yeah, listen uh, I want you to make sure you tell Chandler that he couldn't have been more wrong! Uh-oh! I gotta go Monica, my uh, my sushi's here!

expressions.dialogue: I'll just have a Tic-Tac **to hold me over**.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 진짜 필요한 것이 올 때까지 임시로 버티게 해주다
expressions.nuance: 진짜 원하는 것이 생기기 전까지 임시로 유지하게 해주는 것. 음식에 가장 많이 쓰이지만 돈이나 다른 상황에도 사용 가능하다.
expressions.example: Here's an advance on your paycheck to hold you over until Friday. → 금요일까지 버틸 수 있게 급여 선불로 줄게.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Hey, look Joey, I'm just saying if you need something to hold you over, I can get you a job right here as an entry level processor.

expressions.expression: the mother ship is calling you home
expressions.dialogue: Oh, it's like **the mother ship is calling you home**.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 당신이 자연스럽게 속하는 곳이 당신을 부르고 있다, 드디어 제자리를 찾았다
expressions.nuance: SF 용어 'mother ship(모선)'에서 온 유머러스한 표현. Rachel이 고급 백화점 Sak's 인터뷰를 받자 '그곳이 네 본진'이라는 뜻으로 Phoebe가 쓴 말. 누군가가 자신의 천국 같은 곳에 돌아가는 상황을 재치 있게 표현한다.
expressions.example: You got a job at a bookstore? The mother ship is calling you home! → 서점에 취직했어? 드디어 네 자리를 찾았네!
